title: Dog Frequent Urination of Small Amounts: Triage and Care Guide
meta_desc: Is your dog urinating frequently in small amounts? Learn about pollakiuria, when to see a vet, and how to identify potential urinary emergencies.
content_html:
What is Pollakiuria?

Frequent urination of small amounts, medically referred to as pollakiuria, occurs when a dog feels a constant, urgent need to empty their bladder, even if very little urine has collected. This is different from polyuria, where a dog produces large volumes of urine. Pollakiuria is typically a sign of irritation, inflammation, or an obstruction in the lower urinary tract, such as the bladder or urethra.

Is It an Emergency? (Urgency Level: Medium)

In most cases, frequent urination is a medium-priority concern that requires a veterinary appointment within 24 hours. While it is usually caused by a common urinary tract infection (UTI), it can be quite painful for your pet. However, this becomes a high-priority emergency if your dog is straining and producing no urine at all, as this indicates a life-threatening blockage. If your dog is also vomiting, lethargic, or acting in extreme pain, seek immediate emergency care.

How a Photo Helps Triage

When you call your veterinarian, providing a photo of the urine can significantly assist in the triage process. Capturing the color and clarity of the urine on a light-colored surface (like a sidewalk or a puppy pad) helps the vet see if there is visible blood (hematuria) or cloudiness, which suggests infection or crystals. A photo of your dog’s posture while attempting to go can also help the vet distinguish between simple urgency and dangerous straining.

faqs.question: What are the most common causes of frequent urination?
faqs.answer: The most frequent causes include urinary tract infections (UTIs), bladder stones, urinary crystals, or in older dogs, issues like prostate disease or bladder tumors.

faqs.question: Can I give my dog human UTI medication?
faqs.answer: No, you should never give your dog human medications without veterinary approval. Many human painkillers and supplements are toxic to dogs and can complicate their treatment.

faqs.question: How will the vet diagnose the issue?
faqs.answer: Your vet will likely perform a physical exam and a urinalysis. They may also suggest an ultrasound or X-rays to check for bladder stones or other abnormalities.
